skill.name: gpt-image-2
skill.description: Generate and edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 through the RunComfy CLI. The skill documents supported endpoints, prompt patterns, image sizes, edit preservation guidance, and model routing advice for GPT Image 2 workflows.
skill.summary: Use RunComfy to create and edit GPT Image 2 assets with precise prompts, fixed sizes, and preservation-focused edits.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly found external command, network, and filesystem indicators, but most individual hits are Markdown backticks, command examples, URL placeholders, or prose. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found; the real risk is that the skill directs users to run the RunComfy CLI, send prompts and image URLs to RunComfy endpoints, store or provide a bearer token, and write generated files locally.

medium_findings.title: External RunComfy CLI Invocation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install and run the RunComfy CLI, including commands that submit image jobs and download results. This is expected for the skill, but it is a real external command dependency with network and local output effects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and use the runcomfy executable. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the examples are static CLI invocations and not hidden code execution.

medium_findings.title: Bearer Token Use and Local Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents RUNCOMFY_TOKEN for CI and states that login stores an API token under the user home configuration directory. This is legitimate credential handling, but marketplace users should understand that a local secret is created or supplied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents token storage and environment-token usage. The stated mode 0600 lowers risk, but credential handling remains sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Remote Processing of Prompts and Image URLs
medium_findings.description: The skill sends prompts and image URL references to RunComfy model endpoints for remote processing. This is the intended service model, but it has privacy implications for prompts, reference assets, and generated outputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly states that API calls and remote URL fetching occur. There is no evidence of unexpected endpoints beyond the documented RunComfy service.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are largely Markdown inline code or fenced command examples, not Ruby shell backtick execution. They still identify a CLI-based workflow, but the specific Ruby execution label is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown front matter, tables, and prompt examples. No Ruby source file or executable Ruby syntax is present.

low_findings.title: URL Placeholder Traversal False Positive
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections are URL ellipses such as request status and result paths, not filesystem traversal sequences. No evidence found that the skill reads or writes parent directories through these placeholders.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences appear inside documented HTTP endpoint paths. They are explanatory placeholders and not local file path operations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts appear to match ordinary prose, front matter, headings, or exit-code documentation. No evidence found of cryptographic code, host reconnaissance, or system inventory behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain description metadata, an exit-code heading, and a jq example for request IDs. They do not implement cryptography or reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Service References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy website, documentation, GitHub, model API endpoints, or example public image URLs. This is expected documentation for a hosted image model skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs align with the declared RunComfy integration and model workflow. No unrelated collection endpoint or suspicious third-party callback is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: CLI Commands With User-Supplied JSON Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented runcomfy commands pass prompt data and image URLs through CLI JSON input. The skill states the CLI does not shell-expand prompt content, but users still execute a local command that contacts a remote service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and repeated. The documented JSON boundary reduces command injection concern but does not remove external command risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Output File Side Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow may store a RunComfy token locally and writes generated files to an output directory. These side effects are disclosed, but they require normal filesystem and secret-handling caution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file directly describes token storage, output downloads, and a generated-file size cap. These are expected side effects rather than hidden behavior.

content.user_title: Create GPT Image 2 Assets on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Teams need image prompts that preserve text, layout, and brand details during generation and editing. This skill provides RunComfy commands and prompting guidance for GPT Image 2 workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when GPT Image 2 is the right model for text-heavy and layout-sensitive imagery.
Documents RunComfy text-to-image and edit endpoint names and required inputs.
Provides CLI invocation examples for generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing.
Lists supported size values for text-to-image and edit workflows.
Gives prompt guidance for preserving identity, typography, framing, and composition during edits.
Documents RunComfy CLI behavior for polling, result download, exit codes, and token handling.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a RunComfy account or token.
Uses remote RunComfy model processing, so prompts and reference URLs leave the local machine.
Text-to-image supports only three fixed image sizes.
Multi-image edit guidance is not a precise layer or ControlNet workflow.

use_cases.title: Build product visuals with readable labels
use_cases.description: Create product images where short label text, brand marks, and layout details matter.

use_cases.title: Localize branded image assets
use_cases.description: Generate or edit variants that keep a composition while changing short multilingual text.

use_cases.title: Automate image generation from a terminal workflow
use_cases.description: Use documented RunComfy CLI commands to submit jobs, poll status, and save generated outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple product image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use GPT Image 2 on RunComfy to create a square product image of a matte water bottle on a clean studio background. Include the label text "AQUA+" and save the output locally.

prompt_templates.title: Create a multilingual sign concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Use GPT Image 2 to generate a portrait image of a small cafe storefront at dusk. The sign should read "コーヒー" in bold Japanese kana. Keep the scene warm, legible, and suitable for a brand mood board.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an image while preserving identity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the GPT Image 2 edit endpoint with my reference image URL. Replace the background with a bright studio sweep, add the headline "OPEN STUDIO", and keep the person, pose, face identity, and framing unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Combine multiple references
prompt_templates.prompt: Use GPT Image 2 edit with two reference image URLs. Take the subject from image 1, use the lighting and room style from image 2, and preserve the subject shape and key product details.

output_examples.input: Request: create a product hero image with a short readable label.
output_examples.output: The skill selects the GPT Image 2 text-to-image endpoint, recommends a supported square or portrait size, and provides a RunComfy command pattern with a clear prompt.

output_examples.input: Request: edit a reference photo without changing the person identity.
output_examples.output: The skill uses preservation language for pose, face identity, framing, and brand details, then routes the request to the edit endpoint with the image URL.

output_examples.input: Request: choose between GPT Image 2 and sibling image models.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends GPT Image 2 for embedded text, signage, packaging, UI mockups, and precise layout instructions.

best_practices: Quote exact embedded text and keep it short for better image text rendering.
Use size=auto for edits unless the requested change needs a new frame.
Change one attribute per edit iteration to preserve composition and identity.
anti_patterns: Do not combine conflicting instructions such as no text and a required headline.
Do not send private images or sensitive prompts unless remote processing is acceptable.
Do not request unsupported sizes and expect exact custom aspect ratios.

faq.question: Does this skill call OpenAI directly?
faq.answer: No. It uses the RunComfy CLI and RunComfy hosted model endpoints for GPT Image 2.

faq.question: What image sizes are supported?
faq.answer: Text-to-image supports 1024_1024, 1024_1536, and 1536_1024. Edit also supports auto.

faq.question: Can it edit multiple reference images?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented edit endpoint accepts up to 10 public HTTPS reference image URLs.

faq.question: Where are generated files saved?
faq.answer: The RunComfy CLI downloads generated outputs into the output directory chosen by the user.

faq.question: Does the skill store credentials?
faq.answer: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores a token locally. CI can use RUNCOMFY_TOKEN instead.

faq.question: Is this best for photorealistic portraits?
faq.answer: The skill says GPT Image 2 is strongest for text, layout, and brand precision, not portrait realism.
